title: Transforming TMJ pain relief: Hyaluronic acid's efficacy in focus – a comprehensive systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
research_question: In adult patients diagnosed with TMJ pain and dysfunction, what is the efficacy of infiltrating hyaluronic acid in reducing pain, improving temporomandibular function and preventing complications in comparison to other substances or no intervention?
methods_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs)
Search in Web of Science, Cochrane Library, MEDLINE, PubMed (May 2014–May 2023)
PICOS framework: Population (adult TMJ pain/dysfunction), Intervention (HA injection), Comparison (other materials or no intervention), Outcome (pain relief, MMO, function), Study design (RCTs)
Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane method; evidence quality graded with GRADE
Meta-analysis using fixed-effects model, RR with 95% CI, heterogeneity via I² and χ²
dataset: 14 RCTs from database and manual searches, including studies comparing HA with PRP, corticosteroids, L-PRF, botulinum toxin, tenoxicam, betamethasone, and arthrocentesis alone
key_findings: HA is safe and effective for reducing TMJ pain and improving maximum mouth opening (MMO)
Overall meta-analysis showed no statistical difference between HA and other treatments in improving stomatognathic function (RR = 1.01, 95% CI: 0.92–1.11, p = 0.81, I² = 0%)
HA demonstrated superior long-term results compared to platelet-rich plasma (PRP)
Combination of arthrocentesis with HA injection yielded better chewing efficiency and quality of life than HA alone
HA was more effective than betamethasone or tenoxicam for pain relief, but at higher cost
extracted_insights: Hyaluronic acid is a safe and effective intra-articular treatment for TMJ disorders, particularly for pain reduction and improving mouth opening. While comparable to other injectables in short-term efficacy, HA shows superior long-term outcomes versus PRP. The meta-analysis found no significant heterogeneity across studies, supporting consistency in findings. However, HA's higher cost and the potential superiority of PRP in some contexts should be considered in clinical decision-making.
